title: Bagha Jatin by the Buribalam
subtitle: A quiet river, a small band, and an empire’s shadow
era: Revolutionary nationalism during World War I, 1915
moral: True bravery includes loyalty to companions and responsibility for a larger cause.

sources.citation: Report of the Sedition Committee, 1918 (Rowlatt Report), Government of India, Calcutta, 1918, sections on Bengal revolutionary organisations and the German-Indian conspiracy
sources.note: Official contemporary account of revolutionary networks connected with Jatindranath Mukherjee.

sources.citation: Peter Heehs, The Bomb in Bengal: The Rise of Revolutionary Terrorism in India, 1900-1910, Oxford University Press, 1993
sources.note: Background on Bengal revolutionary organisations that shaped Bagha Jatin's milieu.

sources.citation: Sudhir Kumar Mitra, Bagha Jatin, biographical study, 20th-century Bengali nationalist history
sources.note: Supports the Balasore/Buribalam episode and later remembrance.

dialogue.text: Bagha Jatin, storm-silent by the Buribalam, holds his ground as war and empire close in on a handful of men.
It is 1915. The Great War burns in Europe, and under the flat skies of Bengal, another war moves in shadows and whispers.
Jatindranath Mukherjee, called Bagha Jatin, has spent years weaving threads of revolt—uniting scattered circles, training men, planning beyond the fury of momentary violence.
When war comes, his mind turns to opportunity: German arms by sea, a rising on land, and a blow that might shake the Raj.

dialogue.speaker: bagha_jatin_jatindranath_mukherjee
dialogue.text: We must be ready along the coast. Quietly, carefully.
Across continents, plans twist and tangle—the German-Indian conspiracy noted even by official eyes, as reports in Calcutta begin to speak of smuggled arms and seditious networks.
The net tightens. Informers whisper, telegrams flash, and the Rowlatt records later trace how the state closed in upon these elusive conspirators.
To the police, he is a dangerous organiser, a name in secret files. To his comrades, he is a quiet centre, asking not for glory but discipline.
In September 1915, near Balasore, his band lies low—few in number, with arms scarce, but still bound by a plan larger than their own lives.
By the river Buribalam, the land dips low and quiet. Here, geography offers a thin shield for hunted men.

dialogue.speaker: bagha_jatin_jatindranath_mukherjee
dialogue.text: They are coming. We choose our ground, not our fear.
He knows the odds. The state brings trained men, ammunition, authority. He has a handful of fighters and a stubborn, deliberate will.
Official reports will later note this encounter near Balasore—a brief, fierce clash where a few men stood against a much larger force.
The air is thick with heat and dust. No heroic pose, only men firing, taking cover, moving on instinct and training drilled in secret courtyards.
Even under fire, his thought is not escape alone. It is: who can fall back, who must hold, how to keep the circle of trust unbroken.

panels.shot: medium shot with wounded posture implication

dialogue.speaker: bagha_jatin_jatindranath_mukherjee
dialogue.text: Hold your courage. Think of the work yet to be done.
The fight cannot last. Ammunition runs low. Strength drains away. Between duty to survive and duty to shield his men, he finds no easy path.
Later pages and memorials will embroider last words for him. The record itself is spare: a leader taken, a rebel subdued, the file marked and closed.
Yet in the years that follow, Bagha Jatin’s stand by the Buribalam becomes a story retold—a sign that true bravery binds loyalty to comrades with responsibility to a larger, patient struggle.
